coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with high selectivity for hydrosulfide sensing and their living cells imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Aikun
Cai, Haojie
Zeng, Yutian
Chen, Yu
Yu, Xiantong
Song, Jun
Zeng, Pengju
Qu, Junle
Guo, Jiaqing
Li, Hao
coredata.dc:description: Hydrosulfuric acid is an aqueous solution of hydrogen sulfide (H2S). At physiological pH, approximately 80% of the total amount of H2S exists in the form of monoanionic HS−. Because HS− is both widely distributed and highly toxic to humans, it is necessary to design an efficient method to detect HS− with high sensitivity and selectivity. So, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with green fluorescence are synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared NCDs show it can be effectively used as an indicator for monitoring HS−. And the NCD fluorescence intensity exhibits a linear relationship with HS− concentration. In addition, NCDs as a probe can be applied for fluorescence imaging in living cells to detect the presence of trace exogenous HS−.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
HS−
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of NCDs. (a) TEM image; (b) the size distribution; (c) FTIR spectrum; (d) Full XPS spectrum; (e) C 1s XPS spectrum; (f) N 1s XPS spectrum; (g) O 1s XPS spectrum; (h) UV–Vis absorption (blue curve) and PL spectroscopy (excitation (black curve) and emission (red curve)); (i) Excitation emission 3D fluorescence diagram. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Normalized fluorescence intensity of NCDs (a) various concentrations of NaCl solution; (b) fluorescence intensity at a different time of irradiation; (c) time dependence of fluorescence quenching in the presence of HS−;(d) fluorescence intensity with HS− (red) and without HS− (black) under different pH conditions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Normalized fluorescence intensity of selectivity and interference of NCDs to HS−. (a) Selectivity after adding HS− (200 μM) and other ions (1 mM) respectively; (b) Interference after adding HS− (200 μM) and other ions (1 mM) respectively.

(a) Fluorescence intensity changes of NCDs upon addition of different HS− concentration; (b) Linear fitting curve of NCDs; (c) Fluorescence decay of NCDs and NCDs-HS−; (d) Fitting diagram of the lifetime of NCDs quenched by different concentrations of HS−; (e) Absorption spectrum for the NCDs, HS− and NCDs-HS− solutions; (f) The Zeta potential of NCDs and NCDs-HS−.

(a) Effects of NCDs at different concentrations on the viability of KYSE-150 cells; (b–d) confocal images of living KYSE-150 cells incubated with 50 μg/mL NCDs solution for 10 min. Inside, (b) is the fluorescence field, (c) is the bright field, and (d) overlay image of fluorescence field and bright field; (e–g) confocal images of living KYSE-150 cells incubated with 50 μg/mL NCDs solution mixed with 10 mΜ HS−. Inside, (e) is the fluorescence field, (f) is the bright field, and (g) overlay image of fluorescence field and bright field.

Confocal imaging of exogenous HS− with NCDs (50 μg/mL), fluorescence images at the green channel. (a–c) Incubated with only NCDs for 10 min; (d–f) Pretreated with HS− (5 mM) for 30 min and incubated with NCDs for 10 min. Inside, (a) is the fluorescence field, (b) is the bright field, and (c) overlay image of (a) and (b); (g–i) Pretreated with HS− (10 mM) for 30 min and incubated with NCDs for 10 min. Inside, (g) is the fluorescence field, (h) is the bright field, and (i) overlay image of (g) and (h).. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The synthesis and fluorescence quench of NCDs and the application for intracellular hydrogen sulfide sensing.
Comparison of detection limits and Linear range for the determination of HS− using different probes.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with high selectivity for hydrosulfide sensing and their living cells imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Lab of Optoelectronics Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering
Key Lab of Optoelectronics Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Lab of Optoelectronics Devices and systems of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Research Nuclear University MEPhI (Moscow Engineering Physics Institute), 115409, Moscow, Russian Federation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Research Nuclear University MEPhI (Moscow Engineering Physics Institute)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Research Nuclear University MEPhI (Moscow Engineering Physics Institute), 115409, Moscow, Russian Federation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrosulfuric acid is an aqueous solution of hydrogen sulfide (H2S). At physiological pH, approximately 80% of the total amount of H2S exists in the form of monoanionic HS−. Because HS− is both widely distributed and highly toxic to humans, it is necessary to design an efficient method to detect HS− with high sensitivity and selectivity. So, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with green fluorescence are synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared NCDs show it can be effectively used as an indicator for monitoring HS−. And the NCD fluorescence intensity exhibits a linear relationship with HS− concentration. In addition, NCDs as a probe can be applied for fluorescence imaging in living cells to detect the presence of trace exogenous HS−.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrosulfuric acid is a pungent aqueous solution of hydrogen sulfide (H2S). Approximately 80% of total H2S exists as monoanionic hydrosulfide (HS−) at physiological conditions [1–3]. HS− are considered toxic pollutants and have many harmful effects on human health [4,5]. This is because they can easily diffuse from the solution into the bloodstream and tissues [6], where they cause chronic diseases in the blood, immune, and digestive systems at low concentrations [7], and physiological and biochemical problems like personal pain, suffocation, unconsciousness, and even permanent brain damage at high concentrations [8,9]. Therefore, it is important to find a method to track and accurately quantify HS− in biological systems.

Amongst the various available sensing techniques for HS−, such as electrochemical analysis [10], colorimetry [11], and metal-induced precipitation [12], fluorescence sensing and imaging [13,14] have attracted considerable attention because of simple process, high selectivity, high efficiency, and biological applicability. Further, the use of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon nanomaterial, has emerged as a popular sensing strategy owing to its simple synthesis procedure, low cost, easy-to-obtain raw materials, superior fluorescence properties, good biocompatibility, and good water solubility of these CDs [15–19].

To date, some researchers have reported the use of CDs to detect HS−. Zhuo et al. designed and synthesized Cu-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu-CQDs) using ethylenediaminetetraacetate and copper chloride as precursors. Cu-CQDs have a large detection range of 2–500 μM, and a lower limit of detection (LOD) of 0.5 μM. However, Cu-CQDs’ excitation wavelengths lie within the ultraviolet range near 370 nm, which is phototoxic to organisms and not conducive to biological imaging [20]. And, the probe contains Cu element, so it more to pollute the environment. Zhang et al. obtained a fluorescent probe (CQDs-O-NBD) capable of simultaneously detecting homocysteine (Hcy), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), and HS− by modifying CDs. The best excitation wavelength of the CQDs-O-NBD is 377 nm, which has a good linear relationship in the range of 0–10 μM, and the LOD is 0.18 μM [21]. However, here again, the short excitation wavelength of this CD precludes in vivo applications. Wu et al. developed an abased-N,S-CDs-Hg2+ system as “turn off-on” fluorescence sensing for the detection of sulfide. Under the excitation of 355 nm, the N,S-CDs solution with a FLQY of 16.1%. The limits of detection are 83 nM (0–40 μM) for Hg ions and 11 nM (0–20 μM) for sulfide. In order to detect sulfide in cells, a large amount of exogenous Fe3+ needs to be added to the cells, and the short excitation wavelength is phototoxic to organisms, which is not conducive to biological imaging [22]. Therefore, it is very important to develop CDs that can be excited by long-wavelength for detecting hydrogen sulfide in live cells.

Recently, Pang et al. obtained long-wavelength excited NIR-CDs by one-step hydrothermal method with azure A chloride and copper gluconate as precursors. It was successfully applied to the detection of H2S in cell membrane, and at the NaHS concentration of 0.5–20 μM, the detection limit was as low as 56 nM. But its fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) is only 5%, which requires a large power to excite in the process of cell imaging and causes great damage to cells [23]. Therefore, the development of CDs with high FLQY is more conducive to the detection of intracellular H2S.

Here, we propose green fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) excited by wavelength (513 nm) designed for sensing HS−. The NCDs are composed of tartaric acid and 3-diethylaminophenol precursors and have excellent selectivity, sensitivity, and optical and dispersion properties, with high FLQY, as well as a low LOD. More importantly, the NCDs can perform live-cell imaging and detect exogenous hydrogen sulfide in cells.

The chemicals used in the experiments were purchased from Adamas-beta (Beijing Chemical Reagent) and Sigma-Aldrich. The metal salts (NaHS, CH3COONa, MnCl2⋅4H2O, CaCl2, MgCl2⋅6H2O, BaCl2⋅2H2O, (CH3COO)2Zn, FeCl3⋅6H2O, CuSO4, AgNO3, CsBr, LiCl⋅2H2O, CdCl2, NaF, NaHF2, Na2HPO4, Na2SO3, NaNO2, KH2PO4, NaHCO3, K2CO3, KI, NaH2PO2, Pb(NO3)2), Na2S2O3, NaSCN, GSH, Cys, and Hcy were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). All analytical-grade raw materials were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water was prepared using a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) were performed using an FEI/Philips Tecnai G2F20 TWIN TEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a KRATOS Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a Bruker FTIR spectrometer (Hyperion). Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Horiba Jobin Yvon luminescence spectrometer (Fluoro Max-4). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy was performed using a GBC UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Cintra 2020). The cell imaging was performed using a Laser-scanning confocal fluorescence (Nikon, A1R MP+ and Carl Zeiss, LSM 800 with Airyscan).

Scheme 1 illustrates the preparation procedure, in which 1.0 g tartaric acid, 0.8 g 3-diethylaminophenol, and 30 mL ultrapure water were added to a stainless steel autoclave with polytetrafluoroethylene lining, and heated at 160 °C for 12 h under static conditions. After cooling to 25 °C, a red solution was obtained and filtered with deionized water in a 500-Da dialysis bag to remove impurities, yielding the NCD solution. Ultrapure water was used to disperse NCDs to obtain a 0.01 mg/mL NCDs solution.

The KYSE-150 cells were incubated in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum in a 37 °C incubator, combining 5% CO2/95% air. The cells were placed in a 96-well plate for 24 h, followed by the addition of different concentrations of NCDs. After 24-h cell culture, the cells were washed twice with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) to remove excess NCDs. The KYSE-150 cells were cultured with 10 μL Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) and 100 μL fresh mixed solution, as the medium was added to a 96-well plate and incubated for 1 h. After the color reaction, optical density (OD) was measured at a wavelength of 450 nm using a microplate reader.

The KYSE-150 cells were placed in a cell culture dish (1 mL cell culture medium) and cultured in a cell culture room at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, NCDs (50 μg/mL) were incubated with the KYSE-150 cells at 37 °C for 10 min. The KYSE-150 cells were then washed three times with culture medium, before the addition of 1 mL of DMEM. Finally, we used a Nikon A1R MP + laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope to image the KYSE-150 cells (λ ex = 488 nm; the emission was collected at 500–550 nm).

Firstly, the properties of the NCDs were discussed. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) shows our spherical NCDs with uniform dispersion. The particle size distribution is determined to be 1.5–4.0 nm, with an average diameter of 2.9 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 1b).

Next, the chemical functional groups, elemental compositions, and structures of the NCDs were analyzed by FTIR and XPS. Fig. 1c shows the FTIR spectrum of the NCDs. We attribute the broad absorption peak at 3340 cm−1 to –NH2 and –OH stretching vibrations, the strong peak at 1640 cm−1 to the CO stretching vibration, the peak at 1257 cm−1 to the C–H vibration, the sharp peak at 1131 cm−1 to the C–N–C bond, and the peak at 1072 cm−1 to the C–O–C bending vibration. The above results indicate that nitrogen is successfully incorporated into the NCDs as different forms of nitrogen-containing structures [24].

The XPS spectrum of the NCDs in Fig. 1d shows three characteristic peaks at 284.8, 400.7, and 531.72 eV, corresponding to C, N, and O, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) shows that NCDs have three peaks at 284.88 eV (C–C/CC), 286.38 eV (C–N/C–O), and 288.38 eV (CO) [25]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) shows NCDs have three peaks at 399.08 eV (C–NC), 399.98 eV (C–N), and 401.08 eV (N–H); these peaks prove that the NCDs are nitrogen-doped and that a new carbon-nitrogen structure is formed after the hydrothermal reaction [26]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) shows two peaks at 531.4 eV (CO) and 532.88 eV (C–O).

Moreover, the optical properties of the NCDs were characterized using UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra. Fig. 1h shows three absorption bands at 248, 260, and 274 nm, corresponding to the π→π* transition of CC, the n→π* transition of CO, and the n→π* transition of CN, respectively. In addition, we attribute the peak at 385 nm to the n→π* transition of the CO/C–N bond, and the peak at 513 nm to an excitonic absorption band. These results indicate that the NCDs are conjugated to a high degree [27–30].

The fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of NCDs has a maximum emission peak at 535 nm under excitation at 513 nm. The 3D spectrum in Fig. 1i shows that the fluorescence emission spectrum of NCDs is independent of the excitation spectrum. Further, the NCD exhibit band-gap luminescence, with a quantum yield calculated to be 31.1% (Fig. S1).

In addition, we investigate the stability and the responses to HS− of NCDs as a probe for detecting HS−. Fig. 2 a shows the stability of NCDs measured in a NaCl solution. The NCD fluorescence intensity does not change with NaCl concentrations between 0 and 1 mM, indicating that NCDs are stable in this NaCl concentration range. Fig. 2b shows the effect of exposure time on the fluorescence intensity of the NCD. The NCD fluorescence intensity remains unchanged under UV light (365 nm) for 1 h, indicating that NCDs have photobleaching resistance.

Fig. 2c shows the effect of HS− ions on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs. Immediately following the addition of NCDs to 200 μM HS− solution, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly from the highest intensity 1 to 0.23 within 1 min, and the fluorescence intensity did not change with the increase of reaction time within 60 min. Further, Fig. 2d compares the effects of pH of NCDs solutions with and without HS−. For the NCD solution without HS−, the fluorescence intensity of the NCD solution shows good stability in the pH range of 1–11. At pH > 10, the NCD fluorescence is quenched. For the NCD solution with HS−, the fluorescence intensity remains practically unchanged in the pH range of 1–12. Therefore, NCDs can act as stable and sensitive fluorescent probes for detecting HS−.

Next, we test the selectivity of the NCDs for detecting HS− by independently measuring the fluorescence responses of the NCDs for the following ions and reductive thiolates: Cl−, F−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 − Ac−, H2PO4 −, H2PO2 −, HPO4 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, HF−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, SCN−, S2O3 2−, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Li+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cs+ and GSH, Cys, Hcy. Fig. 3 a compares the NCD fluorescence responses, showing that only the presence of HS− significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 3b also shows that adding the non-HS− ions to the HS− quenched NCDs solution has negligible effect on the quenched NCD fluorescence, indicating that the HS− detection by NCDs is not interfered by the presence of the above ions.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs mainly includes dynamic quenching, static quenching, Föster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and internal filtering effect (IFE) [37–40]. Here, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of HS− triggered NCDs was first explored by measuring the fluorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the lifetime of non-quenched NCDs is calculated to be about 3.48 ns using a single exponential decay trajectory. And with the increase of HS− concentration, the lifetime of NCDs gradually decreased. After 100 μM of HS− was added to the NCDs solution to quench it, the fluorescence lifetime decay curve was obtained, and a short lifetime of about 1.418 ns was obtained by bi-exponential fitting [41]. This eliminates the possibility of static quenching and IFE mechanisms. Then, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of NCDs, HS−, and NCDs-HS− were further tested (Fig. 4e). Unexpectedly, NCDs-HS− showed a new absorption peak at 433 nm. The absorption peak of HS− is at 403 nm, and the absorption peak of the NCDs-HS− system also appears at 403 nm, which means the formation of NCDs-HS− complex [42]. Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of the quencher HS− did not overlap with the emission spectrum of NCDs. It is also shown that the quenching mechanism in the NCDs-HS− system is not dynamic quenching and FRET.

To further study the mechanism of NCDs and HS− interaction. We measured the zeta potential of NCDs and their addition to HS− (Fig. 4f). The results show that the potential of NCDs in aqueous solution is +6.0 mV, while the potential of the NCDs-HS− system is +1.2 mV. It is suggested that NCDs are attracted to HS− by electrostatic interaction. It can be seen from Fig. S2 that the position of the fluorescence emission peak did not change before and after the addition of HS−, indicating that a non-covalent bond was formed between NCDs and HS−. We further tested the change of hydrodynamic diameter and FTIR during quenching. As shown in Fig. S3, we found that after NCDs interacted with HS−, the hydrodynamic diameter (16.34 nm) of NCDs-HS− was much larger than that of NCDs (2.87 nm). The increase in hydrodynamic diameter was due to the formation of complex between NCDs and HS−. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S4), it can be seen that there is no new covalent bond on the surface of NCDs after interaction with HS−. To sum up, it can be considered that the quenching is dependent on the PET mechanism, and a complex is formed between NCDs and HS− through non-covalent bonds that can return to the ground state without emitting photons.

Next, we explore the potential of NCDs for detecting HS− in live biological systems. We use CCK-8 analysis to investigate the toxicity of the NCDs. For this purpose, different concentrations of NCDs are selected and incubated with KYSE-150 cells for 24 h. Fig. 5 a shows that up to the NCD concentration of 100 μg/mL, the cell survival rate is measured to be ∼90%, indicating that the NCDs have little effect on the viability of KYSE-150 cells and are suitable for use as live cell imaging probe.

Next, we demonstrate the use of NCDs for detecting HS− in live cells. We use a confocal microscope to investigate the HS− uptake rate by KYSE-150 cells (Fig. S5). We note that, 1 min before the test, we replace the medium with one that contains NCDs. NCDs gradually migrated to the cell membrane surface, then entered the cell and gradually accumulated in the cytoplasm and nucleolus. The NCD shows nuclear imaging after 3 min, and a good imaging effect is achieved after incubation for 5 min. After 10 min, the fluorescence intensity in the cells did not change to its maximum intensity. This indicates that cellular uptake is a rapid and continuous process.

After incubating the KYSE-150 cells with a medium containing 50 μg/mL NCDs for 10 min, we observe the cells in a channel with an excitation wavelength of 488 nm. Fig. 5b–d show the green fluorescent signal accumulated in the cytoplasm and nucleolus. After adding an exogenous HS− solution to these cells, the intracellular fluorescence is observed to be quenched (Fig. 5e–g), consistent with the in vitro results discussed above. These results are evidence that our NCDs can detect the presence of exogenous HS− in living cells.

To further demonstrate that the ability of NCDs to detect exogenous HS− imaging in KYSE-150 cells involves the quenching of the NCD fluorescence. Fig. 6 a–c show the confocal images of the control group KYSE-150 cells incubated with NCDs only, exhibiting strong green fluorescence. We then expose two batches of KYSE-150 cells to increasing HS− concentrations. We pretreat the first batch with 5 mM NaHS for 30 min, wash it three times with PBS, and then incubate with NCDs (50 μg/mL) for 10 min before imaging (Fig. 6d–f). We pretreat the second batch with 10 mM NaHS under the same conditions before imaging (Fig. 6g–i). Comparison of the images shown in Fig. 6 indicates that an increase in the HS− concentration results in a gradual decrease in the green fluorescence, consistent with the in vitro results discussed above.

Following, the quenching process of NCDs in cells under the action of exogenous HS− is studied. As shown in Fig. S6, after the addition of exogenous HS−, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly at 0.5 min, most of the fluorescence disappeared after 3 min, and the fluorescence is almost completely quenched after 5 min. Owing to the more complex intracellular environment, exogenous HS− can quickly quench the fluorescence of NCDs in cells. The results show that NCDs can visualize exogenous HS− in living cells.

In summary, using the one-step hydrothermal method and the doping effect of N, we have synthesized green fluorescent NCDs with inexpensive and easily available materials. The experimental results show that the new NCDs have excellent quantum yield (31.1%) and stability, high selectivity, and sensitivity, and can be used as a probe for HS− detection. The detection range of HS− by the NCD probe is measured to be 2–55 μM. The detection limit is determined to be as low as 50.11 nΜ. In addition, NCDs can be imaged in living KYSE-150 cells and can successfully detect intracellular HS−.

Aikun Liu: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Haojie Cai: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization. Yutian Zeng: Investigation, Visualization. Yu Chen: Validation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Xiantong Yu: Validation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Jun Song: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Pengju Zeng: Formal analysis, Resources, Project administration. Junle Qu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Jiaqing Guo: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Hao Li: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanistic insights into the H2S-mediated reduction of Aryl Azides commonly used in H2S detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.A. Henthorn, M.D. Pluth, Mechanistic Insights into the H2S-Mediated Reduction of Aryl Azides Commonly Used in H2S Detection, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 137 (48) (2015) 15330-15336.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current approaches for detection of hydrogen sulfide and persulfidation in biological systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Zhao, J. Zhang, M. Zhou, H. Zhou, C. Gotor, L.C. Romero, J. Shen, X. Yuan, Y. Xie, Current approaches for detection of hydrogen sulfide and persulfidation in biological systems, Plant. Physiol. Bioch. 155 (16) (2020) 367-373.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Progress on the reaction-based methods for detection of endogenous hydrogen sulfide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Luo, Y. Zuo, G. Shi, H. Xiang, H. Gu, Progress on the reaction-based methods for detection of endogenous hydrogen sulfide, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 414 (9) (2021) 2809-2839.
R.J. Reiffenstein, W.C. Hulbert, S.H. Roth, Toxicology of Hydrogen Sulfide, Annu. Rev. Pharmacol. Toxicol. 32 (1) (1992) 109-134.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-ion-mediated fluorescent carbon dots for indirect detection of sulfide ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Barati, M. Shamsipur, H. Abdollahi, Metal-ion-mediated fluorescent carbon dots for indirect detection of sulfide ions, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 230 (6) (2016) 289-297.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen sulfide toxicity: mechanism of action, clinical presentation, and countermeasure development

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.C. Ng, T.B. Hendry-Hofer, A.E. Witeof, M. Brenner, S.B. Mahon, G.R. Boss, P. Haouzi, V.S. Bebarta, Hydrogen Sulfide Toxicity: Mechanism of Action, Clinical Presentation, and Countermeasure Development, J. Med. Toxicol. 15 (2019) 287-294.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A synthetic supramolecular receptor for the hydrosulfide anion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.D. Hartle, R.J. Hansen, B.W. Tresca, S.S. Prakel, L.N. Zakharov, M.M. Haley, M.D. Pluth, D.W. Johnson, A synthetic supramolecular receptor for the hydrosulfide anion, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 55 (38) (2016) 11480-11484.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive detection of sulfide based on the self-assembly of fluorescent silver nanoclusters on the surface of silica nanospheres

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yan, K. Zhang, H. Yu, H. Zhu, M. Sun, T. Hayat, A. Alsaedi, S. Wang, Sensitive detection of sulfide based on the self-assembly of fluorescent silver nanoclusters on the surface of silica nanospheres, Talanta, 174 (2017) 387-393.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A β-diketonate–europium(III) complex-based fluorescent probe for highly sensitive time-gated luminescence detection of copper and sulfide ions in living cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen sulfide consumption measured at low steady state concentrations using a sulfidostat
